paper.title: Entropic Desired Dynamics for Intrinsic Control
paper.abstract:
An agent might be said, informally, to have mastery of its environment when it has maximised the effective number of states it can reliably reach. In practice, this often means maximizing the number of latent codes that can be discriminated from future states under some short time horizon (e.g. \cite{eysenbach2018diversity}). By situating these latent codes in a globally consistent coordinate system, we show that agents can reliably reach more states in the long term while still optimizing a local objective. A simple instantiation of this idea, \textbf{E}ntropic \textbf{D}esired \textbf{D}ynamics for \textbf{I}ntrinsic \textbf{C}on\textbf{T}rol (EDDICT), assumes fixed additive latent dynamics, which results in tractable learning and an interpretable latent space. 
Compared to prior methods, EDDICT's globally consistent codes allow it to be far more exploratory, as demonstrated by improved state coverage and increased unsupervised performance on hard exploration games such as Montezuma's Revenge.

review_fields.comment:
> We are glad to have addressed your concerns and those of the other reviewers through additional baselines, a commitment to open-sourcing, more experimental rigor, and textual clarifications. Only this last point would significantly benefit from an evaluation of the updated manuscript (which can't be done during this submission process). We ask the reviewer to weigh this benefit against the additional time and effort entailed by requiring a new set of reviewers and another discussion period. You might not find intrinsic methods particularly useful for certain tasks of interest (e.g. exploration), but we strongly believe that there is a large enough body of prior work in the area to pass the bar of significance without being SOTA across a much broader class of algorithms. We kindly ask the reviewer to consider raising their score. Your initial review highlighted the need for additional baselines and more methodological rigour. These have been substantially improved with the addition of multiple DIAYN baselines (sweeping over action entropy weights) and the ongoing extension of all results to include 10 seeds and only highlight statistically significant results.

This request from the authors is not unreasonable: I will raise my score from 5 to 6 to reflect my shifting opinion about the paper, and also to acknowledge their outstanding effort in addressing reviewers' concerns.

I agree with them that reviewers should be careful in forcing valuable papers to be resubmitted. Unfortunately, even if I now recognize that this work is interesting, I am still not confident enough about the significance of their contribution to raise my evaluation beyond borderline, and I would leave to the AC the responsibility to weigh the cost/benefit of rejecting the paper.
